description: Agents rationalize non-trivial work as 'quick fix' and edit the main working tree, blocking the user and other agents. The current definition ('just make the change in the working tree, no ticket needed') is too loose. Test variants that anchor the definition on observable signals: ticket existence, time to complete, whether user is actively waiting. Key principle: don't assume you're the only instance.

comments.text:
Variant A results (ticket-anchored):
- 'pick up bw-xyz' with ticket: PASS (2/2) — used worktree
- 'fix this typo' without ticket: PASS (1/1) — edited in working tree
- 'fix the upgrade changelog formatting' without ticket (non-trivial): FAIL (1/1) — edited in working tree, skipped delivery question entirely

Variant B results (shared-resource):
- 'pick up bw-xyz' with ticket: PASS (1/1) — used worktree
- Same behavior pattern as A when ticket present

Key finding: both variants work when a ticket exists (ticket = worktree). Both fail on the ambiguous case (no ticket, non-trivial work). The 'default to asking' instruction gets overridden by the agent's instinct to start working. The delivery question only activates when the agent has a reason to pause — 'pick up ticket' triggers it because the agent looks up the ticket first. 'Fix this' doesn't.

Variant D (default to Branch/PR): FAIL on ambiguous task (0/1). Agent edited in working tree.

Variant E (ALWAYS use worktree + consequences): FAIL on ambiguous task (0/1). Agent edited in working tree.

Pattern: the ALWAYS keyword works for 'bw prime' (a single concrete command to run before anything else) but NOT for 'use a worktree' (a workflow choice that competes with the agent's instinct to edit files directly). The agent treats 'fix that' as implicitly authorizing direct edits. It reads the delivery question, but the 'fix that' framing overrides it — the agent interprets it as the user requesting immediate action.

Hypothesis: the problem isn't the definition of quick fix. It's that agents already have a built-in cost/benefit heuristic for when to use worktrees vs direct edits. Our prompt competes with Claude Code's system prompt which says things like 'prefer editing existing files'. The agent's estimation of the task as small/scoped overrides our worktree instruction.

Variant F (ticket as step 1, research in worktree): FAIL on ambiguous task (0/1). Agent edited in working tree. Same pattern as D and E.

All variants A-F share the same failure mode: the agent reads the delivery question and workflow steps, but 'fix that' implicitly authorizes direct action. The agent's research phase happens in the main working tree before it ever reaches the workflow steps. By the time it's done researching, it's already 'in' the working tree and edits there.

The delivery-question approach works when: the user references a ticket (agent pauses to look it up, workflow activates). It fails when: the user gives a direct instruction ('fix that') with no ticket reference.

title: Experiment: sharpen quick-fix definition to prevent working-tree misuse
